preFix.title: Repairing a crack in a solid plaster wall
preFix.subtitle: Open, undercut, and fill a hairline or medium crack in lime or gypsum plaster
preFix.excerpt: How to repair a crack in a solid plaster wall so it doesn't re-open. Covers the difference between surface cracks and movement cracks, the undercut technique that locks the filler in, and the correct painting sequence.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Traditional UK plastering practice.

content.text: Most cracks in plaster walls are cosmetic. A hairline crack across a flat section of plaster is the finish coat moving slightly over years; it fills easily and stays filled. A crack that runs along a lintel or diagonal from a door corner is a
, it will re-open through rigid filler. Identify which before filling.

Run a finger along the crack. A hairline on a flat wall section is surface movement only. A crack wider than 2 mm, or one that follows a structural junction (the line of a lintel, a diagonal off a door corner), needs flexible filler (a silicone or acrylic decorator's caulk) rather than patching plaster, rigid filler in a movement crack cracks again within months.
Step 2, Open and undercut the crack

Do not fill a hairline crack as it stands, the filler sits in a slot with parallel sides and can pop out cleanly when stressed. Open the crack to a width of 3 to 5 mm using the tip of the utility knife or the corner of the filling knife, then
both sides: angle the knife into the crack and draw it along each side so the slot is slightly wider at the back than at the face. Blow or brush out all loose dust.

Dampen the crack with a paint brush and clean water. Dry plaster sucks moisture out of the filler too quickly and causes shrinkage cracks as it dries. For deep cracks (over 10 mm), apply a little diluted PVA (1:5 with water) and let it become tacky before filling.

For deep cracks, fill in two passes: fill to within 3 mm of the surface on the first pass and let it dry before the second. One-coat lightweight filler shrinks slightly as it dries, filling to full depth in one pass leaves a hollow when set. Press the filler firmly into the undercut with the 50 mm knife, working it into the back of the crack. Draw the 100 mm knife across the surface to level, leaving the repair slightly proud by 0.5 to 1 mm. Sand flush when dry.

Sand the repair with 120-grit paper on the sanding block, working along the crack direction. Seal with a mist coat of white emulsion thinned 10% before applying the finish coat, bare filler is more absorbent than the surrounding painted wall and will show as a patch of different sheen if painted without priming first.
Movement crack at a structural junction: use decorator's acrylic flexible caulk in a cartridge gun. Smooth with a wet finger, leave to cure, and paint over.

Cracks in lime plaster (older properties): use a lime-compatible filler rather than gypsum-based patching plaster. Gypsum over lime is less of a concern on small repairs than on large areas, but matching the base material gives better long-term results.

items.fix: Remove the filler, apply flexible acrylic caulk, and allow full cure before painting.
items.cause: Movement crack filled with rigid patching plaster instead of flexible caulk

items.fix: Overfill slightly on the second coat and sand back when dry. Deep repairs always need two passes.
items.cause: Deep crack filled in one pass; filler shrinks as it sets
items.symptom: Filler sinks below the wall surface as it dries
